name: New Jersey
regulatoryOwner: New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection
delegatedLocalAuthorities: Certified closure contractors and NJDEP cleanup workflows drive the answer, even when local records are thin.
buyerSellerSummary: A missing heating-oil tank record during a sale may keep real transaction risk open until the parties verify tank presence or closure paperwork.
recordLookupSummary: Start with permits, closure documents, and prior heating-fuel history before treating silence as proof that no tank exists.
sweepFirstSummary: A sweep is often the honest first step when old fill or vent evidence appears but the file does not close the question.
removalVsAbandonmentSummary: The disposition path depends on confirmed tank conditions, site access, and the NJDEP heating-oil tank workflow.
leakReportingSummary: A suspected release may move the case from ordinary closure into reporting, remediation, and no-further-action review.
insuranceCleanupSummary: Cleanup and reimbursement questions may follow a different timeline from simple locate or closure work.
quickAnswer: In New Jersey, buyers and sellers usually need to verify records and likely tank presence before debating price or removal.
commonTriggers: Old fill or vent pipes appear before closing.
Seller paperwork is silent on prior oil heat or closure history.
Inspection notes raise a buried tank question without confirming a tank.
cautions: Do not assume a missing record proves there is no tank.
Do not blur a routine closure question with a release or remediation question.
Do not jump to firm cost talk before a sweep or document review.
strongestTriggerStates: The buyer or seller has no closure document, but old oil-heat evidence is still visible on site.
An inspection note or sweep result suggests a tank while the closing timeline is still live.
The file starts as a transaction question, then release language or cleanup history appears.
firstMoves: Pull seller disclosure, prior oil-heat conversion records, and any closure paperwork before talking about credits or removal.
If old fill or vent evidence remains, use a sweep or locate to decide whether the route is still only suspicion.
If the file mentions a release, fund claim, or no-further-action review, tighten the route around cleanup facts rather than generic removal talk.
documentTargets: Seller disclosure packet, permits, and contractor closure records tied to prior oil heat.
Any NJDEP case reference, fund paperwork, or no-further-action language.
Fuel conversion records, removal invoices, or transfer files that explain when the system changed.
escalationSignals: Odor, staining, or excavation history suggesting the case is no longer only a missing-record problem.
Any state paperwork that references a release, remediation, or no-further-action decision.
A contractor or inspection note that confirms tank presence or questions contamination, not just tank age.
